titleStmt.title: Letter from Celia Duffin to Maria Duffin, April 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Celia Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Celia Duffin (1888-1979) to her mother Maria (1854-1954) informing her that she is recovering quickly from her recent illness and letting her know her latest news.
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Thursday
Nov. 1915 Dearest Mamma Just a line to say that I have
recovered very rapidly and my neck is
nearly well again, disappearing as
quickly as it came. It was a rheumatic
chill of some sort with a temperature
for two days. I am quite normal
to-day and as it was nice and sunny
here to-day, saw the doctor and have
been out for a walk. I am now
back in my own billetts and have
lit a nice fire. I found a nice parcel from
malcolmson's waiting for me to-day,
thanks so much, it has turned up at a
very opportune moment and I am
going to have a good tea. I am afraid I have missed
my day off which I was going to
spend at Forest Will, but may
get it later instead unless this is
counted as a holiday. I haven’t got your Sunday letter
yet, as two were waiting for me at
the hospital till last night when one
of the nurses posted them to my
temporary lodgings, so I have just
missed them again, but will call
for them to-morrow. I saw Olive on Monday evening,
looking very well, but I am afraid
getting very bored with Eastbourne. She
and Hilda had been to the theatre
and H: was resting in bed afterwards. I think I must have got my
chill going home in the underground. Have you heard anything more
from D ? I am longing to hear all
news. Thank you again for sending
the rug. Best love
from Celia
